text: Incident postmortem: Checkout latency after release v42. The affected service was checkout-api. Alerts included: P1: checkout-api p95 latency above 2400 ms for 8 minutes checkout-api container memory at 93 percent and rising payment-worker queue depth elevated but stable. Relevant logs included: 10:02 deploy release=checkout-api:v42 commit=9af31d2 actor=deploy-bot 10:04 warn heap_growth_mb=512 route=/cart/quote feature=recommendations 10:06 error oom_risk request_id=c-1842 tenant=retail-east 10:07 warn gc_pause_ms=821 route=/checkout feature=recommendations 10:09 info rollback_candidate release=checkout-api:v41 healthy_baseline=true 10:05 warn queue_depth=184 reason=upstream checkout-api latency 10:08 info retries within budget; no payment processor errors 10:06 warn processor_latency_ms=510 provider=paygate-west possible_external_issue=true 10:07 error transient_payment_decline code=issuer_unavailable sample_size=3 10:06 warn db_pool_wait_ms=380 endpoint=/orders/create below_slo_threshold=true. Relevant metrics: checkout-api.latency_p95_ms moved from 180 to 3100; checkout-api.memory_percent moved from 48 to 95; checkout-api.error_rate moved from 0.01 to 0.17; payment-worker.queue_depth moved from 12 to 201; payment-worker.error_rate moved from 0.0 to 0.01; payment-worker.processor_latency_ms moved from 120 to 490; orders-api.db_wait_ms moved from 60 to 380. The root cause was bad_deploy_memory_leak. Safe remediation options were: rollback_checkout_api_v42, disable_checkout_recommendations_flag. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: restart_primary_database, purge_all_sessions. Causal chain: checkout-api:v42 recommendations rollout -> heap growth and long GC pauses -> checkout-api latency and OOM risk -> payment-worker queue backs up as a downstream symptom. Plausible but misleading causes were: payment processor outage, orders db pool exhaustion. Stakeholder impact: Checkout is slow for a subset of retail-east users; payments are delayed but not failing.

Runbook for checkout-api: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for checkout-api, orders-api, payment-worker. Query logs for terms such as checkout-api:v42, heap_growth_mb, memory, gc_pause_ms, recommendations, rollback_checkout_api_v42. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to bad_deploy_memory_leak. Prefer rollback_checkout_api_v42 when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as payment-worker queue, retail-east tenant and validate the origin chain: checkout-api:v42 recommendations rollout -> heap growth and long GC pauses -> checkout-api latency and OOM risk -> payment-worker queue backs up as a downstream symptom.

Stakeholder communication for Checkout latency after release v42: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Checkout is slow for a subset of retail-east users; payments are delayed but not failing.

Incident postmortem: Orders API saturation during inventory sync. The affected service was orders-api. Alerts included: P1: orders-api 5xx rate above 8 percent database connection wait time above 1200 ms inventory-sync retries climbing. Relevant logs included: 14:21 error db_pool_timeout pool=orders-main waited_ms=1500 14:22 warn active_connections=80 pool_max=80 endpoint=/orders/create 14:23 error request_failed reason=db_pool_timeout trace=o-771 14:25 info config_change inventory-sync batch_size=5000 14:20 info started full catalog reconciliation batch_size=5000 14:24 warn retrying order reservation due upstream orders-api 503 14:22 warn heap_growth_mb=220 route=/cart/quote unrelated_to_orders=true 14:24 info release=checkout-api:v42 canary_healthy=true 14:25 warn full_catalog_reconciliation still_running suspected_batch_issue=true. Relevant metrics: orders-api.error_rate moved from 0.01 to 0.12; orders-api.db_wait_ms moved from 80 to 1900; orders-api.cpu_percent moved from 42 to 62; inventory-sync.retry_count moved from 0 to 144; inventory-sync.throughput moved from 900 to 850; inventory-sync.cpu_percent moved from 52 to 70; checkout-api.memory_percent moved from 45 to 61. The root cause was db_connection_pool_exhaustion. Safe remediation options were: increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: drop_orders_read_replica, disable_all_inventory_sync. Causal chain: inventory-sync batch size increase -> orders-api saturates orders-main connection pool -> db wait time and 5xx rise on order creation -> inventory-sync retries increase as a downstream symptom. Plausible but misleading causes were: checkout memory leak, inventory sync outage. Stakeholder impact: New order creation is intermittently failing; inventory sync is a trigger, not the root fault.

Runbook for orders-api: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for checkout-api, inventory-sync, orders-api. Query logs for terms such as db_pool_timeout, active_connections=80, pool_max=80, db_wait_ms, increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to db_connection_pool_exhaustion. Prefer increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as inventory-sync retries, catalog reconciliation and validate the origin chain: inventory-sync batch size increase -> orders-api saturates orders-main connection pool -> db wait time and 5xx rise on order creation -> inventory-sync retries increase as a downstream symptom.

Stakeholder communication for Orders API saturation during inventory sync: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: New order creation is intermittently failing; inventory sync is a trigger, not the root fault.

Incident postmortem: Profile service cache stampede. The affected service was profile-service. Alerts included: P2: profile-service latency above 900 ms cache hit rate dropped below 35 percent user-feed showing elevated dependency latency. Relevant logs included: 09:41 config ttl_seconds=0 source=feature_flag profile-cache-expiry 09:43 warn cache_miss_rate=0.72 keyspace=user_profile 09:44 warn duplicate_rebuilds key=user:483 count=48 09:45 info dependency user-feed slow because profile-service latency 09:43 warn evictions normal memory_percent=57 09:45 info cluster healthy nodes=6 09:44 error feed_render_timeout component=ranking possible_root=true 09:45 warn ranking_model_latency_ms=780 but dependency_wait_ms=1210 09:44 warn cache_eviction_spike keyspace=session count=900 unrelated_keyspace=true. Relevant metrics: profile-service.latency_p95_ms moved from 180 to 1240; profile-service.cache_hit_rate moved from 0.93 to 0.25; profile-service.origin_qps moved from 400 to 3400; cache.memory_percent moved from 55 to 57; cache.evictions moved from 12 to 13; cache.session_evictions moved from 20 to 880; user-feed.ranking_latency_ms moved from 110 to 760. The root cause was cache_ttl_misconfiguration. Safe remediation options were: restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight, rate_limit_profile_rebuilds. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: flush_all_profile_cache, scale_down_cache_cluster. Causal chain: profile cache TTL feature flag sets ttl_seconds=0 -> profile-service cache hit rate collapses -> origin QPS and duplicate rebuilds spike -> user-feed latency rises through dependency calls. Plausible but misleading causes were: user-feed ranking regression, cache cluster memory pressure. Stakeholder impact: Profile reads are slow and user-feed is degraded through dependency latency.

Runbook for profile-service: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for cache, profile-service, user-feed. Query logs for terms such as ttl_seconds=0, cache_miss_rate, duplicate_rebuilds, cache_hit_rate, restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to cache_ttl_misconfiguration. Prefer restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as cache evictions, user-feed latency and validate the origin chain: profile cache TTL feature flag sets ttl_seconds=0 -> profile-service cache hit rate collapses -> origin QPS and duplicate rebuilds spike -> user-feed latency rises through dependency calls.

Stakeholder communication for Profile service cache stampede: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Profile reads are slow and user-feed is degraded through dependency latency.

Incident postmortem: Auth service certificate expiry. The affected service was auth-service. Alerts included: P1: login success rate below 70 percent edge-gateway upstream TLS failures to auth-service support tickets mention intermittent login loops. Relevant logs included: 18:02 error upstream_tls_handshake_failed service=auth-service verify=certificate_expired 18:03 warn retrying upstream auth-service status=525 18:05 info canary route unaffected service=marketing-site 18:01 info cpu_percent=34 memory_percent=41 18:04 warn no application exceptions in auth handlers 18:06 info cert_subject=auth.internal expires_at=2026-04-25T12:00:00Z 18:03 warn slow_query_ms=640 table=sessions below_incident_threshold=true 18:04 info connection_pool healthy active=32 max=120 18:05 warn password_reset_errors count=4 possible_user_report_noise=true. Relevant metrics: auth-service.login_success_rate moved from 0.98 to 0.61; auth-service.cpu_percent moved from 30 to 35; auth-service.memory_percent moved from 39 to 42; edge-gateway.tls_failures moved from 0 to 420; edge-gateway.upstream_525 moved from 0 to 281; oauth-database.query_p95_ms moved from 80 to 610. The root cause was expired_tls_certificate. Safe remediation options were: rotate_auth_service_certificate. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: disable_tls_verification_globally, restart_oauth_database. Causal chain: auth-service certificate expires -> edge-gateway TLS handshakes to auth-service fail -> login success rate drops -> support sees intermittent login loops. Plausible but misleading causes were: oauth database timeout, password reset regression. Stakeholder impact: Some users cannot log in because edge traffic cannot establish TLS to auth-service.

Runbook for auth-service: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for auth-service, edge-gateway, oauth-database. Query logs for terms such as upstream_tls_handshake_failed, certificate_expired, expires_at, tls_failures, rotate_auth_service_certificate. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to expired_tls_certificate. Prefer rotate_auth_service_certificate when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as login loops, auth database and validate the origin chain: auth-service certificate expires -> edge-gateway TLS handshakes to auth-service fail -> login success rate drops -> support sees intermittent login loops.

Stakeholder communication for Auth service certificate expiry: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Some users cannot log in because edge traffic cannot establish TLS to auth-service.

In Checkout latency after release v42, payment-worker can appear suspicious and may suggest payment processor outage. Treat that as a red herring unless it explains checkout-api evidence: checkout-api:v42 heap_growth_mb memory gc_pause_ms recommendations checkout-api:v42 recommendations rollout heap growth and long GC pauses. The correct root cause is bad_deploy_memory_leak.

Cascading chain for Checkout latency after release v42: checkout-api:v42 recommendations rollout -> heap growth and long GC pauses -> checkout-api latency and OOM risk -> payment-worker queue backs up as a downstream symptom. Fix the origin, not only the downstream symptom. The safe fix is rollback_checkout_api_v42.

A good Incident Commander answer is a compact JSON array of 6 to 9 tool calls. It ends with finish_incident and stops immediately after the closing bracket. It does not include markdown, prose, or repeated plans.

For checkout-api, the communicator must say 'investigating' before root cause is confirmed, and only say 'bad_deploy_memory_leak' or 'rollback_checkout_api_v42' after the team has evidence and remediation is deployed.
